sexual_orientation.definition_in_act: Sexual orientation - means heterosexuality, homosexuality or bisexuality

gender_identity.definition_in_act: Gender history - means the status of having identified, or having been identified, as a member of the opposite sex by living, or seeking to live, as a member of the opposite sex
gender_identity.notes: WA uses the term 'gender history' with binary language ('opposite sex') which does not clearly protect non-binary or gender diverse people. Law Reform Commission recommended updating this but reforms not implemented as at 31 Dec 2025.

intersex_status_or_sex_characteristics.source: Equal Opportunity Act 1984 (WA) - no intersex/sex characteristics protection
intersex_status_or_sex_characteristics.notes: WA does not protect intersex people or people with variations in sex characteristics. Law Reform Commission recommended adding this protection in 2022 but reforms not implemented as at 31 Dec 2025.

areas_where_discrimination_is_unlawful: Provision of goods, services and facilities
areas_excluded_or_limited: Religious organisations have very broad exemptions
No positive discrimination provisions for transgender people (unlike for sexual orientation)

religious_organisations.summary: Very broad religious exemptions allow religious organisations to discriminate against LGBTIQ+ people in almost all circumstances, including employment and service provision. The WA Law Reform Commission recommended narrowing these exemptions (similar to Victoria's approach) in 2022, and the WA Government committed to implementing reforms, but legislation has not been passed as at 31 Dec 2025.

small_employers.summary: No small employer exemption in WA

other_exemptions.type: Dress and appearance standards (gender history only)
other_exemptions.summary: Employers can discriminate on ground of gender history to enforce standards of dress and appearance

complaint_body.name: Equal Opportunity Commission of Western Australia
complaint_body.time_limit: 12 months from the act of discrimination (extensions may be granted)
complaint_body.costs_summary: No fee for lodging a complaint

statutory_basis: Equal Opportunity Act 1984 (WA), Part IXB - racial harassment and racial vilification only
vilification.notes: WA has vilification laws for race only. There are NO vilification protections for LGBTIQ+ people as at 31 December 2025. The WA Law Reform Commission recommended introducing LGBTIQ+ vilification provisions in 2022 but reforms have not been implemented.
vilification.source: Equal Opportunity Act 1984 (WA); Alastair Lawrie analysis

how_to_contact: Call 131 444 (Police Assistance Centre) and ask for an LGBTI Liaison Officer
Visit your local police station and request an LGBTI Liaison Officer

key_points_summary: Sexual orientation and gender history are protected, but protections are limited
Gender history uses binary language ('opposite sex') which does not clearly protect non-binary or gender diverse people
Intersex people and people with variations in sex characteristics are NOT protected
Very broad religious exemptions allow religious organisations to discriminate in almost all circumstances
No vilification protections for LGBTIQ+ people (only race protected)

WA Law Reform Commission recommended major reforms in 2022 (add sex characteristics, update gender identity definition, narrow religious exemptions, add vilification protections) and Government committed to implementing them, but no legislation passed as at 31 Dec 2025

sexual_orientation.what_this_means: You cannot be discriminated against because you are heterosexual, gay, lesbian, or bisexual.
real_world_examples: Your employer cannot fire you for being gay, lesbian, or bisexual
A landlord cannot refuse to rent to you because you're in a same-sex relationship
A hospital cannot deny you treatment based on sexual orientation
A café cannot refuse to serve you because you're in a same-sex relationship
sexual_orientation.limitations: Asexual people are not explicitly protected. Very broad religious exemptions mean religious organisations can still discriminate in many circumstances.

gender_identity.what_this_means: You cannot be discriminated against if you are transgender with a binary identity (male or female). Non-binary people are NOT clearly protected.
real_world_examples: Your employer must use your correct name and pronouns if you're a trans man or trans woman
You cannot be denied employment because you are transitioning from male to female or female to male
A landlord cannot refuse housing because you are a trans man or trans woman
gender_identity.limitations: WA uses binary language ('opposite sex') which does NOT protect non-binary or gender diverse people. Employers can discriminate to enforce dress and appearance standards. Very broad religious exemptions. Law Reform Commission recommended updating definition in 2022 but reforms not implemented.

intersex_status.what_this_means: Intersex people and people with variations in sex characteristics are NOT protected in WA.
real_world_examples: An employer CAN ask invasive questions about your body if you're intersex
A school CAN exclude you from activities because of sex characteristics
A gym CAN refuse you membership because you are intersex
intersex_status.limitations: WA does NOT protect intersex people or people with variations in sex characteristics. Law Reform Commission recommended adding this protection in 2022 but reforms not implemented as of December 2025.

what_youre_protected_from: Being rejected for a job because you're gay/lesbian/bisexual or trans (binary only)
Being paid less than colleagues because you're LGBTIQ+
Being harassed by coworkers or managers
Being fired because you come out as gay/lesbian/bisexual or trans
not_protected_from: Discrimination by religious organisations (very broad exemptions)
Dress and appearance standards (employers can discriminate against trans people to enforce these)
Discrimination if you're asexual, non-binary, or intersex (unclear or no protection)

what_youre_protected_from: Public schools refusing to enroll your child because you're a same-sex couple
not_protected_from: Religious schools can discriminate against LGBTIQ+ students and staff (very broad exemptions)

what_youre_protected_from: Landlords refusing to rent because you're gay/lesbian/bisexual or trans (binary)
Being evicted because of your sexual orientation or gender identity
Real estate agents refusing to show you properties
Being refused service at a café or restaurant (if you're gay/lesbian/bisexual or binary trans)
A public hospital refusing to treat you
Hotels refusing to book rooms for same-sex couples
not_protected_from: Discrimination by religious organisations (very broad exemptions)

what_youre_protected_from: Being excluded from a sports team because you're gay/lesbian/bisexual or trans (binary)
Being denied membership in a professional association

religious_organisations.plain_english_summary: WA has very broad religious exemptions. Religious organisations can discriminate against LGBTIQ+ people in almost all circumstances.
what_they_can_do: Religious schools can refuse to hire LGBTIQ+ teachers
Religious schools can expel LGBTIQ+ students
Religious organisations can refuse to provide services to LGBTIQ+ people
Religious hospitals can refuse to hire LGBTIQ+ staff
what_they_cannot_do: Very little - the exemptions are extremely broad in WA
religious_organisations.real_example: A Catholic school can refuse to hire a gay teacher or expel a trans student. A religious hospital can refuse to employ a lesbian nurse.
religious_organisations.if_you_face_discrimination: WA has weak protections. The Law Reform Commission recommended narrowing religious exemptions (similar to Victoria) in 2022 and the Government committed to reforms, but no legislation has been passed. Seek legal advice from Equal Opportunity Commission WA.

jurisdiction_rating.rating_explanation: WA has weak LGBTIQ+ protections. Binary-only gender identity definition doesn't protect non-binary people. Intersex people are NOT protected. Very broad religious exemptions. No vilification protections. Law Reform Commission recommended major reforms in 2022 and Government committed to implementing them, but no legislation passed as of December 2025.
strengths: Sexual orientation (gay, lesbian, bisexual) and binary trans people have some protections
gaps: Binary-only gender identity definition ('opposite sex') does NOT protect non-binary or gender diverse people
Very broad religious exemptions allow religious organisations to discriminate in almost all circumstances
No vilification protections for LGBTIQ+ people (only race protected)
Employers can discriminate against trans people to enforce dress and appearance standards

Law Reform Commission recommended major reforms in 2022 (add sex characteristics, update gender identity definition, narrow religious exemptions, add vilification protections) and Government committed to implementing them, but no legislation passed as of December 2025

secondary_analysis: Alastair Lawrie - A Quick Guide to Australian LGBTI Anti-Discrimination Laws
